elements.sent-2411-001: Symplectic and Poisson geometry emerged as a tool to understand the mathematical structure behind classical mechanics.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, due to its huge development over the past century, it has become an independent field of research in differential geometry.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this lecture notes, we will introduce the essential objects and techniques in symplectic geometry (e.g Darboux coordinates, Lagrangian submanifolds, cotangent bundles) and Poisson geometry (e.g symplectic foliations, some examples of Poisson structures).
elements.sent-2411-004: This geometric approach will be motivated by examples from classical physics, and at the end we will explore applications of symplectic and Poisson geometry to Lie theory and other fields of mathematical physics.
elements.sent-2411-005: These notes are based on the four lectures of the mini-course taught by the first and third author at Universidad Nacional de Colombia (July 22-26, 2024) during the first ECOGyT (``Encuentro Colombiano de Geometr\'ia y Topolog\'ia"), and by the first author at University of Notre Dame (June 17-21, 2024), during the Undergraduate Workshop as part of the Thematic Program in Field Theory and Topology.
elements.sent-2411-006: The audience for these two events was quite diverse, ranging from undergraduate math students, and graduate students in different fields besides geometry, to physics students.
elements.sent-2411-007: For this reason, the lectures were intended to introduce the field with a small glimpse to recent results, and to motivate a deeper understanding for those interested in the field of mathematical physics.
elements.sent-2411-008: The first step of the lectures is to motivate and to define the Poisson bracket associated with the dynamics of Hamiltonian systems.
elements.sent-2411-009: Later on we will see that this bracket also encodes the geometric structure of the so called Poisson manifolds, the main object of study in Poisson geometry.
elements.sent-2411-010: After studying the main features of Poisson geometry, symplectic manifolds will appear as a natural example, since all symplectic manifolds are Poisson.
elements.sent-2411-011: As an illustrative example, the phase space of a physical system (the space with physical momentum and position as coordinates) is a symplectic manifold, and it is equipped with a natural Poisson bracket that is used to study of the equations of motion.
elements.sent-2411-012: This manuscript is not intended to cover a whole course on Poisson geometry or Hamiltonian systems, but we aim to introduce the basics of the subject to motivate the reader to delve deeper into this area and its related topics on mathematics or physics (or both).
elements.sent-2411-013: Symplectic and Poisson geometry can be extended beyond physics and geometry.
elements.sent-2411-014: There are numerous connections with other fields of mathematics, and some of such connections are depicted in the diagram below, which is not as complete as it is desirable, just because connections with other fields and areas are continuously increasing in number and complexity to be included in one single diagram.
elements.sent-2411-015: For consistency, throughout this work, we will assume that all configuration spaces are finite-dimensional manifolds, except for certain special cases that will be discussed in the first section.
elements.sent-2411-016: Additionally, all functions, vector fields, and tensors are considered smooth.
elements.sent-2411-017: For more on symplectic geometry, the references \cite{Cannas,WBQuanti,LSM,ps are highly recommended, and for more details on Poisson geometry, see \cite{PoissCFM, dufour2005poisson, laurent-gengoux2013poisson .}}

elements.section-2411-003: The background geometry for Hamiltonian systems
elements.sent-2411-073: In the previous section, we have seen instances of Hamiltonian systems that are essentially different.
elements.sent-2411-074: For instance, the Euler top has non-trivial Casimir functions, while the system with potential force only has trivial (i.e constant) Casimir functions.
elements.sent-2411-075: This leads us to begin with a simplified version of the Hamiltonian system and its geometry: the case of trivial Casimir functions, that are known as symplectic Hamiltonian systems.
elements.sent-2411-076: In this section, we will focus on the geometric framework essential for the formal description of Hamiltonian systems.
elements.sent-2411-077: We begin by introducing the symplectic structure on a vector space, setting the foundation for its generalization to non-linear structures, namely manifolds.
elements.sent-2411-078: Following this, we explore Poisson geometry, which arises as a degenerate extension of symplectic structures.
elements.sent-2411-079: We will examine methods for constructing Poisson structures through foliations and highlight the significance of the Lie-Poisson structure in physical applications.
elements.sent-2411-080: In the previous section, we expressed Hamilton's equations \ref{hje using a skew-symmetric matrix \( J_0 \), which, as we will show, is the matrix representation of what is known as a symplectic form on a vector space.}
elements.sent-2411-081: We will begin by examining the fundamental properties of symplectic structures in the context of vector spaces.
elements.sent-2411-082: Subsequently, we will introduce their non-linear counterparts on manifolds, known as symplectic manifolds, where these structures find broader and more intricate applications, such as a generalization of classical mechanics for any phase space.
elements.sent-2411-083: For simplicity, we refer to \cite{Cannas, mcduff_salamon, SympWeinstein and the references therein as standard sources for this section.}
elements.sent-2411-084: For the purpose of these notes, we restrict ourselves to vector spaces over $\mathbb R$.
elements.sent-2411-085: Let $V$ be a vector space over $\mathbb R$.
elements.sent-2411-086: A skew-symmetric form $\omega \in \Lambda^2(V^*)$ is called non-degenerate \index{non degenerate form if the induced linear map \begin{eqnarray}\omega^{\#}\colon V \to V^* \omega^{\#}(v)(w) := \omega(v,w)\end{eqnarray} is an isomorphism, where $V^*$ denotes the linear dual space of $V$.}
elements.sent-2411-087: In this case, $\omega$ is called {\it symplectic form and $(V,\omega)$ a symplectic vector space.}
elements.sent-2411-088: A more relaxed version of non-degeneracy is required for infinite-dimensional vector spaces.
elements.sent-2411-089: A form $\omega$ is called weakly non degenerate \index{weakly non degenerate if $\omega^{\#}$ is an injective map.}
elements.sent-2411-090: A bilinear form $\omega$ on $V$ is called weak symplectic \index{weak symplectic if it is skew-symmetric and weakly non-degenerate.}
elements.sent-2411-091: A vector space $V$ equipped with a weakly symplectic form $\omega$ is called a weakly symplectic vector space\index{weakly symplectic vector space .}
elements.sent-2411-092: Observe that, in the finite-dimensional case, the facts of $\omega^{\#}$ being bijective and $\omega$ being non degenerate are equivalent.

elements.sent-2411-420: To conclude these notes, we offer a quick overview of another example of how symplectic and Poisson geometry appear naturally in physics.
elements.sent-2411-421: The Poisson sigma model (PSM) is a topological field theory that corresponds to the following data:.
elements.sent-2411-422: A compact surface $\Sigma$, possibly with boundary, called the source.. \index{source.}
elements.sent-2411-423: A finite dimensional Poisson manifold $(M,\Pi)$, called the target.. \index{target.}
elements.sent-2411-424: The space of fields for this theory is denoted with $\Phi$ and corresponds to the space of vector bundle morphisms of class $\mathcal C^{k+1}$ between $T\Sigma$ and $T^*M$.
elements.sent-2411-425: This space can be parametrized by a pair $(X, \eta)$, where \begin{equation}X\in \mathcal C^{k+1}(\Sigma, M)\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\eta \in \Gamma^k(\Sigma, T^*\Sigma \otimes X^*T^*M),\end{equation} and $k \in \{0,\,1,\, \cdots \, , \infty\}$ denotes the regularity type of the map that we choose to work with.
elements.sent-2411-426: On $\Phi$, the following first order action is defined: \begin{equation}S(X,\eta):= \int_{\Sigma} \langle \eta,\, dX\rangle+ \frac 1 2 \langle \eta, \, (\Pi^{\#}\circ X) \eta \rangle,\end{equation} where, \begin{eqnarray}\Pi^{\#} \colon T^*M\to TM \psi \mapsto \Pi(\psi, \cdot).\end{eqnarray}.
elements.sent-2411-427: In the action functional, $dX$ and $\eta$ are regarded as elements in $\Omega^1(\Sigma, X^*(TM))$, $\Omega^1(\Sigma, X^*(T^*M))$, respectively and $\langle \,,\, \rangle $ is the pairing between $\Omega^1(\Sigma, X^*(TM))$ and $\Omega^1(\Sigma, X^*(T^*M))$ induced by the natural pairing between $T_xM$ and $T_x^*M$, for all $x \in M$.
elements.sent-2411-428: Cattaneo and Felder \cite{cattaneo-felder:poissonsigma proved that the reduced phase space of the Poisson Sigma Model, if smooth, when the surface is a disk, has the structure of a symplectic groupoid.}
elements.sent-2411-429: For more recent work on the connections bewteen PSM and symplectic groupoids, see \cite{Cattaneo2014RelationalSG, Contreras2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-430: This model also has significant importance for deformation quantization \cite{kontsevich2003deformation .}
elements.sent-2411-431: Namely, the perturbative expansion through Feynman path integral for PSM, in the case that $\Sigma$ is a disk, gives rise to Kontsevich's star product formula, i.e. the semiclassical expansion of the path integral \begin{equation}\int_{X(r)=x}f(X(p)) g (X(q))\exp{(\frac{i}{\hbar} S(X,\eta)) dX d\eta}\end{equation} around the critical point $X(u)\equiv x, \eta \equiv 0$, where $p,q$ and $r$ are three distinct points of $\partial \Sigma$, corresponds to the star product $f\star g (x)$.. \bibliographystyle{abbrv.}
